title: Painting a room: walls and ceiling
subtitle: Cut in, roll, two coats
excerpt: The full sequence for redecorating a room's walls and ceiling in emulsion: preparation, cutting in at the edges, rolling the field, drying between coats, and reading the result in the right light.
sourceNotes: Standard UK decorating practice documented in Collins Complete DIY Manual and the City & Guilds Painting and Decorating syllabus. Paint application sequence and cutting-in technique unchanged from Victorian trade practice described in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

recipeTools.notes: For tight window-bar and cornice detail.
For filling any cracks or nail holes before painting.
For rubbing back any raised areas or old drips.
For removing any loose or flaking paint.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of painting a clean edge along skirting, architrave, ceiling line, or window-bar with a brush, before rolling the bulk of the wall. A 50 mm sash brush held at the tip, not the ferrule, gives the cleanest line.

content.text: Painting a room is the most common decorating job in any house. The tools are cheap, the materials are simple, and the difference between a poor result and a good one comes down entirely to preparation and patience, not skill. This article covers one coat of emulsion on walls and ceiling, then a second coat the following day. It assumes the walls are sound and previously painted. Bare plaster, new plasterboard, and heavily stained walls each need priming first.
What you'll need
Emulsion paint.

Matt for ceilings, matt or silk for walls depending on preference. One litre of standard emulsion covers roughly 12 to 14 square metres in a single coat. Measure walls and ceiling, divide by 12, and buy two litres extra for safety.
50 mm sash brush.
For
cutting in
around skirting, architrave, and ceiling line. Synthetic bristle for water-based paint; it washes out without turpentine.
9-inch roller and frame with a medium-pile sleeve.
The medium pile (10 to 12 mm) covers the paint on textured or lightly blown walls without over-loading. A short-pile sleeve for smooth walls, long pile for heavy texture.
Paint tray.
Line it with a disposable tray liner if available. The ribbed section lets you roll out excess so the sleeve doesn't drip.
Decorator's tape, 25 mm.
Mask the top of skirting boards and the edge of window frames. Remove before the paint dries to avoid lifting it.
Cotton dust sheet.
Cover the floor and any remaining furniture. Plastic sheeting is slippery underfoot; a cotton sheet holds splatter where plastic lets it pool.
Preparation
Step 1, clear and cover the room.

Move furniture to the centre and cover it with the dust sheet. Lay a second sheet on the floor around the perimeter. Take down any curtain rails, light fittings, and switch plates you can reach safely. Wipe the walls with a barely damp cloth to remove dust and grease. Grease repels water-based paint and leaves a patchy, uneven finish.
Step 2, fill and sand any defects.

Press lightweight filler into any cracks, holes, or nail pops with the filling knife. Allow it to cure fully, usually several hours or overnight. Sand the filled areas level with 180-grit on the sanding block. Wipe off the dust.
Step 3, apply masking tape.
Run decorator's tape along the top edge of skirting boards and along the outer face of door frames and window architrave. Press the tape edge firmly with a fingernail so paint cannot bleed under it.
Painting the ceiling
Step 4, cut in the ceiling edge.

Load the 50 mm brush to about two-thirds of the bristle length and press off the excess on the rim of the tin. Hold the brush near the tip, not the ferrule, and paint a 70 mm band along the junction of ceiling and wall, working in 600 mm sections. The first stroke lays the paint; the second stroke, nearly dry, smooths it flat.
Step 5, roll the ceiling field.

Load the roller in the tray, rolling out the excess on the ribbed section until the sleeve is evenly loaded but not dripping. Work in one-metre squares, rolling in parallel stripes, then crossing at right angles to even the coverage. Work back into the cut-in edge while it is still wet so there is no visible join. Do not over-roll. Emulsion dragged when it is half-dry streaks badly.
Painting the walls
Step 6, cut in the wall edges.

Work around the room: brush the top wall edge against the ceiling, down into the corners, along the skirting top, and around every architrave. The cut-in band needs to be wet when the roller comes in alongside it, so cut one wall at a time, then immediately roll it.
Step 7, roll the wall.

Roll from ceiling height to floor height in parallel stripes, working across the wall in one-metre sections. Finish each section with a light, nearly-dry roller pass from top to bottom to remove any lap marks. Remove the masking tape before the paint dries.
The second coat

Emulsion dries to the touch in 30 to 60 minutes but does not take a second coat without dragging until it has cured for at least 2 hours; in a cold or damp room, wait 4 hours. The second coat follows exactly the same sequence as the first. Most emulsion paints achieve full opacity in two coats. A third coat is occasionally needed over a strong colour change.
Reading the result

Check the finished walls in raking light from a window or a side lamp: position a light source at an angle to the wall rather than face-on. This reveals any missed patches, roller marks, or brushed areas where the sheen differs from the rolled surface. Touch in these areas with the roller while the paint is still in the tray.
What it looks like when it's right

Uniform sheen across the whole wall, no lap marks between roller sections, and clean edges where wall meets ceiling and where wall meets skirting. The cut-in brush marks disappear into the rolled surface.
Common mistakes
Patchy areas on the finished wall.
Usually caused by rolling over half-dried paint. Roll one wall at a time without pausing. If the paint has dried before you finish the wall, a second coat resolves it.
Visible brush marks at the cut-in edge.
The roller did not come far enough into the cut-in while it was wet. Correct in the second coat by rolling to within 10 mm of the edge.
Paint under the masking tape.
The tape was not pressed flat at the edge, or it was left on until the paint cured. Pull the tape at 45 degrees while the paint is still slightly tacky.
Variations
Ceiling only.
Mask the top of the walls with tape, paint ceiling first, then walls. Avoids having to cut in a second time.
Feature wall in a different colour.
Mask the adjoining walls at the corners. Paint the feature wall first, let it dry, remove the tape, mask over the feature wall edge, and paint the remaining walls.
